message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Bangladesh is going through an unprecedented crisis since the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic. Throughout the COVID-19 pandemic, the reproduction number of COVID-19 swarmed in the scientific community and public media due to its simplicity in explaining an infectious disease dynamic. This paper aims to estimate the effective reproduction number (Rt) for COVID-19 over time in Bangladesh and its districts using reported cases.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Adapted methods derived from Bettencourt and Ribeiro (2008), which is a sequential Bayesian approach using the compartmental Susceptible-Infectious-Recovered (SIR) model, have been used to estimate Rt.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>As of July 21, the mean Rt is 1.32(0.98-1.70, 90% HDI), with a median of 1.16(0.99-1.34 90% HDI). The initial Rt of Bangladesh was 3, whereas the Rt on the day of imposing nation-wide lockdown was 1.47, at the end of lockdown phase 1 was 1.06, at the end of lockdown phase 2 was 1.33. Each phase of nation-wide lockdown has contributed to the decline of effective reproduction number (Rt) for Bangladesh by 28.44%, and 26.70%, respectively, implying moderate effectiveness of the epidemic response strategies.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Interpretation and Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The mean Rt fell by 13.55% from May 31 to July 21, 2020, despite easing of lockdown in Bangladesh. The Rt continued to fall below the threshold value one steadily from the beginning of July and sustained around 1. The mean Rt fell by 13.55% from May 31 to July 21, 2020, despite easing of lockdown in Bangladesh. As of July 21, the current estimate of Rt is 1.07(0.92-1.15: 90% HDI), meaning that an infected individual is spreading the virus to an average of one other, with 0.07 added chance of infecting a second individual. This whole research recommends two things- broader testing and careful calibration of measures to keep Rt a long way below the crucial threshold one.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Highlights</jats:title>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>As of July 21, the mean Rt and growth factor is 1.32 and 1.02, respectively.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Each phase of nation-wide lockdown has contributed to the decline of effective reproduction number (Rt) for Bangladesh by 28.44%, and 26.70%, respectively, implying moderate effectiveness of the epidemic response strategies.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>The Rt of Bangladesh was below 1 for only 20 days, which was observed during May 24- 25, June 19-21, from June 30 to July 6, July 9-12, and July 16-19,2020.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>The initial Rt of Bangladesh was 3, whereas the Rt on the day of imposing nation-wide lockdown was 1.47, at the end of lockdown phase-1 was 1.06, at the end of lockdown phase-2 was 1.33.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>The mean Rt fell by 13.55% from May 31 to July 21, 2020, despite easing of lockdown in Bangladesh.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>The Rt continued to fall below the threshold value one steadily from the beginning of July and sustained around one.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>We suspect that a low testing rate may influence the constant decline of Rt below threshold value 1 in the course of July.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>The mean Rt fell by 13.55% from May 31 to July 21, 2020, despite easing of lockdown in Bangladesh.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>As of July 21, the current estimate of Rt is 1.07(0.92-1.15: 90% HDI), meaning that an infected individual is spreading the virus to an average of one other, with 0.07 added chance of infecting a second individual.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: Estimation of Effective Reproduction Number for COVID-19 in Bangladesh and its districts

reference.article-title: Estimation of Time-Dependent Reproduction Numbers for Porcine Reproductive and Respiratory Syndrome across Different Regions and Production Systems of the US

reference.unstructured: Nishiura H , Chowell G. The Effective Reproduction Number as a Prelude to Statistical Estimation of Time-Dependent Epidemic Trends. Mathematical and Statistical Estimation Approaches in Epidemiology. 2009;103–121.
